title: CoSetProxyBlanket and CoInitializeSecurity should be avoided
description: "CoSetProxyBlanket" and "CoInitializeSecurity" should not be usedAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalsecurityVulnerabilityWhy is this an issue?More InfoCoSetProxyBlanketandCoInitializeSecurityboth work to set the permissions context in which the process invoked immediately after is executed. Calling them from within that process is useless because it’s too late at that point; the permissions context has already been set.Specifically, these methods are meant to be called from non-managed code such as a C++ wrapper that then invokes the managed, i.e. C# or VB.NET, code.Noncompliant code example[DllImport("ole32.dll")] static extern int CoSetProxyBlanket([MarshalAs(UnmanagedType.IUnknown)]object pProxy, uint dwAuthnSvc, uint dwAuthzSv
recommendation: Remove the problematic code and refactor as needed.

logic.detection_conditions: Usage or declaration of CoSetProxyBlanket or CoInitializeSecurity in managed C# code
logic.triggering_conditions: (CoSetProxyBlanket OR CoInitializeSecurity) AND (method call OR DllImport declaration) AND NOT in native/unmanaged wrapper
logic.exclusion_conditions: Exclude comments, documentation, native C++ code references
logic.reasoning: CoSetProxyBlanket and CoInitializeSecurity must be called from unmanaged code before managed code execution. Calling them from within managed C# code is useless because the permissions context is already set. The rule detects both DllImport declarations and calls to these methods.

should_not_trigger: // Use C++ wrapper to call CoSetProxyBlanket
